content: PTY and remote terminal security pitfalls: (1) Terminal escape sequence injection (ANSI/VT sequences) — data from remote machine can contain sequences that execute commands, change terminal title, trigger hyperlink clicks in some terminals; this is CWE-150 and enables RCE via victim's terminal emulator; (2) xterm.js demo has no security measures — any JS code on the host page can read keystrokes including passwords; XSS = shell access; (3) PTY session not cleaned up on WebSocket disconnect can leave orphaned processes consuming server resources and potentially continuing to execute; (4) Terminal resize events (SIGWINCH) sent by attacker-controlled client can crash PTY subprocess; (5) Binary data corruption when mixing UTF-8 and binary output in PTY stream; (6) Session isolation failure — if PTY sessions share process space, signal injection from one session can affect others; (7) No command logging means no audit trail of what was executed remotely.
